<answer>
The mascots known as Fuwa were introduced during the 2008 Beijing Summer Olympics. These five adorable characters—Beibei, Jingjing, Huanhuan, Yingying, and Nini—each represented specific elements related to the event and Chinese culture. Their introduction marked a significant cultural moment, symbolizing the spirit and hospitality of China as it welcomed athletes and visitors from around the globe.

The official motto of the 2008 Beijing Summer Olympics was "One World, One Dream." This powerful phrase encapsulated the core values that the Games aimed to promote: global unity, peace, mutual understanding, and the aspiration for excellence in sport. It conveyed the hope that athletes and people from every corner of the world could come together to share their dreams, support one another, and celebrate humanity’s achievements, regardless of differences in nationality, language, or background.

The choice of this motto reflected both the international character of the Olympic Games and the unique opportunity for China to showcase its progress, openness, and cultural heritage on a global stage. The message of "One World, One Dream" resonated not only within the host city but across millions of people watching worldwide, serving as a rallying cry for inclusiveness and shared aspirations.

In summary, the Olympics that featured Fuwa as mascots—the 2008 Beijing Summer Olympics—were characterized by the motto “One World, One Dream.” This slogan remains closely associated with those Games, highlighting themes of unity, collaboration, and the universal pursuit of excellence.</answer>
